claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 20 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Charlie is 5 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 20, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 13 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you some questions.

Let's start with the basics:

**Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you looking to claim Child Benefit for?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have three children - Alex, Blake, and Charlie. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! You have three children: Alex, Blake, and Charlie. 

To help determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for each of them, I need to gather some information about your circumstances.

**Let me start by asking: How old are Alex, Blake, and Charlie?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is 20, Blake is 16, and Charlie is 5.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

Since Alex is 20 years old, I need to ask: **Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** 

(Child Benefit normally continues for children under 20 if they're in approved education or training, but stops at age 16 otherwise.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that about Alex.

Now let me ask about Blake, who is 16: **Is Blake currently in approved education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake left school recently, but they signed up with the careers service to figure out what to do next.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful information. You mentioned Blake left school recently and registered with the careers service. 

**How many weeks ago did Blake leave education?** (This is important because you can claim Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education and registers with a government-sponsored careers service.)

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Now, regarding your living situation and responsibilities:

**Do all three children (Alex, Blake, and Charlie) currently live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex and Charlie live with me, but Blake doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Blake doesn't live with you, I need to ask:

**Are you contributing financially towards Blake's upkeep (such as for food, clothes, pocket money, or accommodation) in an amount at least equal to the Child Benefit payment?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not currently contributing financially for Blake.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Now I need to confirm a few more details:

**Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. Just to confirm one final detail:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for any of these three children (Alex, Blake, or Charlie)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for any of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Based on what you've told me, here's the outcome for each of your children:

## **Alex - Not Eligible** ❌
Alex is 20 years old and not in approved education or training. Child Benefit normally only continues for children aged 16-19 if they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex is 20 and not in education or training, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

## **Blake - Not Eligible** ❌
Blake is 16 and left education approximately 20 weeks ago. Although Blake registered with the careers service, Child Benefit can only continue for up to 20 weeks after a 16 or 17 year old leaves education if they register with a government-sponsored careers service. Since it has now been about 20 weeks, this extension period has ended. Additionally, Blake does not live with you and you are not contributing financially towards Blake's upkeep, which means you do not meet the responsibility test. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

## **Charlie - Eligible** ✅
Charlie is 5 years old, which is under the age limit of 16. Charlie lives with you, which means you are responsible for Charlie. You live in the UK and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. Therefore, **you are eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie**.

---

**In summary:** You can claim Child Benefit for Charlie, but not for Alex or Blake based on your current circumstances.

reasonings: Alex is 20 years old and not in approved education or training. Child Benefit normally only continues for children aged 16-19 if they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex is 20 and not in education or training, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 16 and left education approximately 20 weeks ago. Although Blake registered with the careers service, Child Benefit can only continue for up to 20 weeks after a 16 or 17 year old leaves education if they register with a government-sponsored careers service. Since it has now been about 20 weeks, this extension period has ended. Additionally, Blake does not live with you and you are not contributing financially towards Blake's upkeep, which means you do not meet the responsibility test. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie is 5 years old, which is under the age limit of 16. Charlie lives with you, which means you are responsible for Charlie. You live in the UK and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has three children but is only eligible for Child Benefit for one of them. Alex (age 20) is too old and not in education/training. Blake (age 16) is at the edge of the 20-week extension period after leaving education, does not live with the claimant, and the claimant makes no financial contributions. Only Charlie (age 5) meets all the eligibility criteria as they are under 16, live with the claimant, and no one else is claiming for them.

Since Alex is 20 and not in education or training, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 16 and left education approximately 20 weeks ago. Since it has now been about 20 weeks, this extension period has ended. Additionally, Blake does not live with you and you are not contributing financially towards Blake's upkeep, which means you do not meet the responsibility test. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie is 5 years old, which is under the age limit of 16. Charlie lives with you, which means you are responsible for Charlie. You live in the UK and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie.

Blake (age 16) is at the edge of the 20-week extension period after leaving education, does not live with the claimant, and the claimant makes no financial contributions. Only Charlie (age 5) meets all the eligibility criteria as they are under 16, live with the claimant, and no one else is claiming for them.
